title: Matthew 4
subtitle: The Temptation of Jesus; The Call of the First Disciples; Ministry in Galilee

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Temptation in the Wilderness

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — The Temptation: Three Tests in the Wilderness

heb.paragraph: The same verb means both 'tempt' (from the devil's perspective — to entice to sin) and 'test' (from God's perspective — to prove character). The Spirit leads Jesus into the wilderness to be 'tested'; the devil arrives to 'tempt.' Same event, two opposing purposes.

heb.gloss: it is written / it stands written
heb.paragraph: Jesus responds to each temptation with this perfect passive: 'it stands written' — the force of the perfect tense is that Scripture was written in the past and remains authoritative in the present. Jesus fights Satan not with miraculous power but with the permanent authority of the written word.

hist.context: The temptation narrative is structured as a Deuteronomy exam. Jesus has just been declared God's Son at the baptism; Satan now attacks that identity three times with "if you are the Son of God." Each test targets a different dimension of messianic identity: provision (bread/Messiah as miracle-worker), protection (temple/Messiah as wonderworker), power (kingdoms/Messiah as military conqueror). Jesus answers each with Deuteronomy, recapitulating Israel's wilderness story as the obedient Son where Israel was disobedient.

refs.note: The three texts Jesus quotes — all from the section of Deuteronomy addressing Israel's wilderness failures. Jesus is passing the test Israel failed, from the same textbook.
Satan quotes Scripture (correctly) to support the second temptation. The misuse of Scripture in service of testing God is the diagnostic of the second temptation.
Israel's 40 years of wilderness wandering — bread complaints, water testing, idolatry. Jesus's 40 days recapitulates all three failures and passes them all.

echoes.source_context: Three passages from Deuteronomy 6–8, all from Moses' speeches recounting Israel's wilderness failures: not living by bread alone, not testing God, worshiping God alone.
echoes.connection: Jesus answers each of Satan's three temptations with a quotation from Deuteronomy — the same book that records Israel's wilderness failures. Where Israel failed each test over forty years, Jesus passes each test in forty days.
echoes.significance: The typological pattern is precise: Israel was God's 'son' (Exodus 4:22) tested in the wilderness for forty years and failed. Jesus is God's Son tested in the wilderness for forty days and succeeds. He recapitulates Israel's story and gets it right. The new Israel begins with faithful obedience where the old Israel stumbled.

poi.name: Wilderness of Judea
poi.coords: East of Jerusalem toward the Dead Sea
poi.text: Jesus is led into the wilderness for forty days — the geography of Israel's forty years of testing. The temptation in the wilderness is the final recapitulation of Israel's wilderness failure: Jesus succeeds where Israel failed, in the same geographical type of space.

poi.name: Holy city pinnacle
poi.text: The second temptation places Jesus on the pinnacle of the Temple in Jerusalem — the geographic and theological centre of Jewish identity. The test is whether he will use his identity to command spectacular rescue at the most sacred site.

poi.name: High mountain
poi.coords: Traditional: Mount of Temptation near Jericho
poi.text: From a high mountain "all the kingdoms of the world and their splendor" are visible — a supernatural geography that transcends topography. The mountain is the space of vision and decision: from here, the scope of what is being refused becomes visible.

notes.note: Calvin: These three temptations comprehend all satanic assaults. The first is against trust in God's provision; the second against trust in God's protection; the third against worship of God alone. Every temptation resolves into one of these three. Christ conquers each by Scripture — teaching us that God's word is our only sufficient weapon.

Calvin on 'man shall not live on bread alone': Christ quotes Deut 8:3, where Moses reminds Israel that God fed them with manna to teach that life depends on the divine word, not on material provision alone. He will not use miraculous power to provide for himself outside the Father's appointed means.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the forty-day fast discusses the typological significance: Moses fasted forty days on Sinai (Exod 34:28); Elijah fasted forty days at Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8); Israel wandered forty years. Jesus' forty days recapitulate Israel's wilderness experience, succeeding where Israel failed. Each temptation is met with a Deuteronomy quotation.

The NET note on the kingdoms as the devil's to offer discusses the theological question of Satan's current authority (Luke: 'to me it has been delivered') and identifies this as the temptation to bypass the cross — achieving the universal kingdom by shortcut rather than by suffering and resurrection.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson notes that 'led up by the Spirit' (anēchthē hypo tou pneumatos) is a divine passive — the Spirit actively directs Jesus into the wilderness for the purpose of being tempted. The forty days echo Moses (Exod 34:28) and Elijah (1 Kgs 19:8), placing Jesus in the covenant mediator tradition.

Robertson observes that Jesus does not deny the devil's authority over the kingdoms — the temptation is real. The offer is the immediate crown without the cross. Jesus' third Deuteronomy quotation establishes that the worship of God alone is non-negotiable regardless of what is offered in exchange.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom: The Spirit led Jesus into the wilderness to give opportunity for the devil to attempt all his arts. God permitted it so that the one who would arm us against the devil should first overcome him in solitude and then go forth to fight him openly in the world.

Hilary of Poitiers: The devil offers the kingdoms of the world to him who is Lord of all creation. The audacity of the temptation reveals the tempter's blindness — he does not know whom he is tempting. Christ refuses not from weakness but from the refusal to take by any means other than the cross what is rightly his.

sections.header: Verses 12–17 — Ministry Begins in Galilee: Light in Darkness

heb.paragraph: Matthew quotes Isaiah 9:1-2: 'the people living in darkness have seen a great light.' Jesus' relocation to Capernaum in Galilee fulfils this prophecy. The region dismissed by Jerusalem elites ('Galilee of the Gentiles') becomes the place where messianic light first dawns.

Jesus begins with the same message as John (3:2): 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven has come near.' The verb kēryssō denotes a herald's public announcement — authoritative, urgent, demanding response. Jesus does not suggest or invite; he proclaims.

hist.context: The move to Galilee (vv.12–17) fulfils Isaiah 9:1–2 and inaugurates the public ministry. Galilee of the Gentiles — the northernmost, most cosmopolitan region — is the location Matthew chooses for the first proclamation. The kingdom begins at the margins, not the centre. Jesus's first words (v.17) are identical to John's first words (3:2): the continuity is deliberate. The forerunner has been arrested; the one he prepared the way for now steps forward.

refs.note: Land of Zebulun and Naphtali; people in darkness see a great light — the OT context is the Assyrian devastation of the northern tribes. Matthew reads it as fulfilled by Jesus's Galilean ministry: light dawns on the most oppressed region first.
Repent, for the kingdom of heaven has come near — Jesus's first words are verbatim John's. The continuity of the message is as important as the discontinuity of the messenger.

poi.name: Galilee
poi.coords: Northern Israel; Sea of Galilee region
poi.text: "When Jesus heard that John had been put in prison, he withdrew to Galilee." The withdrawal to Galilee fulfils Isaiah 9:1-2 — light dawning in the land of Zebulun and Naphtali. Jesus begins his ministry where the prophets said the light would break.

poi.name: Capernaum
poi.coords: Fishing town on northwest shore of Sea of Galilee
poi.text: Jesus settles in Capernaum — his ministry base. Capernaum is in the territory of Zebulun and Naphtali, the "Galilee of the Gentiles." The choice of a Galilean lakeside town (not Jerusalem) as the base of operations continues the pattern of covenant grace at the margins.

notes.note: Calvin: Christ begins with the same proclamation as John — repentance is the gateway to the kingdom. This is not a legal demand but a gracious invitation: the one who announces the kingdom is the one who provides what the kingdom requires. He commands repentance and enables it by his own presence.

Calvin on the disciples' immediate response: The promptness of their obedience was not natural but supernatural — Christ drew them by a hidden, powerful call working in their hearts simultaneously with the outward word. We should not imagine every disciple must literally abandon occupation and family; but this total availability is the disposition genuine discipleship requires.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Jesus beginning with John's words (3:2) identifies deliberate parallel: Matthew signals continuity between the forerunner and the one foretold. The message is the same because the reality is the same — the kingdom has arrived in John's announcement and now in the King himself.

The NET note on 'teaching, proclaiming, healing' traces this triad through Matthew (4:23; 9:35; 11:1) as the structural description of the Son of Man's mission. The healing vocabulary — therapeuōn combined with pasas nosos (every disease) — establishes the comprehensive character of Jesus' ministry.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson notes Jesus' withdrawal to Galilee after John's arrest is strategic wisdom. His settlement in Capernaum fulfils Isa 9:1-2 ('land of Zebulun and Naphtali') — the most Gentile-influenced region of Israel. Jesus begins his ministry in the darkness's own territory.

Robertson describes 'teaching in their synagogues' (edidasken) and 'proclaiming the gospel' (kēryssōn) as two distinct activities: synagogue teaching expounds Scripture to the covenant community; public proclamation announces the kingdom to all. The summary of 4:23 will be repeated at 9:35 as a literary bracket.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Jerome: He who is himself the light of the world chose to begin in the region Isaiah called the land of darkness. The fulfilment of Isaiah 9 shows that Gentile inclusion was not an afterthought — it was planned from the beginning. Galilee of the Gentiles receives the light before Jerusalem.

Chrysostom on the call of the disciples: They left their nets, their ship, and their father without delay. They did not say let us settle our affairs first. They forsook everything and followed — the model for every call to discipleship. The kingdom admits no delay, no negotiation.

sections.header: Verses 18–25 — The First Disciples; Teaching, Proclaiming, Healing

poi.name: Sea of Galilee
poi.coords: Freshwater lake in northern Israel, 21km x 13km
poi.text: The first disciples are called beside the Sea of Galilee. Simon and Andrew are casting a net; James and John are in a boat with their father. The fishing geography is transformed: "I will make you fishers of people." Their vocation becomes their metaphor.

poi.name: Syria, Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, Transjordan
poi.text: The summary of Jesus's early ministry reach (vv.24-25) is a geographical statement: crowds from all over the Levant come to him. Syria to the north, Decapolis (ten Greek cities east of the Jordan), Jerusalem and Judea to the south, Transjordan to the east. The ministry is not geographically confined to Israel.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament -- Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson on the call of the four fishermen: the immediacy of the response (they left the boat and their father and followed him, v.22) is Robertson's text for the authority of the call. No argument, no deliberation, no delay -- the call of Jesus produces immediate response.

Robertson on the summary statement of Jesus's Galilean ministry (v.23): teaching in their synagogues, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom, healing every disease. The threefold description -- teaching, proclaiming, healing -- is Matthew's recurring summary formula (cf. 9:35) that frames the major teaching and healing sections.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas, Catena Aurea (Golden Chain) -- Public Domain

notes.note: The Catena records Chrysostom's meditation on the fishermen's call: they left their nets immediately. Chrysostom: they did not say let us go home and consult our families; they did not hesitate because their livelihood was being abandoned. The greatness of the caller explains the greatness of the response.

The Catena traces the summary of Jesus's Galilean ministry as the programmatic description of the messianic mission. Chrysostom: the healing of every disease and sickness is not selective -- it demonstrates comprehensive authority over the physical consequences of the fall.

calvin.source: John Calvin, Commentary on the Harmony of the Evangelists -- Public Domain

notes.note: Calvin on the fishermen leaving their nets: they left not merely their occupation but their father -- the bonds of filial duty. This teaches that when Christ calls, no natural tie is stronger than the call. The immediate obedience is not imprudent haste but the recognition of absolute authority.

Calvin on the summary of Jesus's Galilean ministry: the three activities (teaching, proclaiming, healing) are not separate functions but one unified mission. Teaching establishes the kingdom's character; proclaiming declares its arrival; healing demonstrates its power.

netbible.source: NET Bible Notes -- Scripture quotations marked (NET) are from the NET Bible

notes.note: The NET note on 'fishers of men' (halieis anthropon, v.19) discusses the metaphor: fishing involves catching, drawing out of one element into another. The note traces the positive connotation (rescue rather than death) and the professional competence the metaphor implies: these men know how to catch.

The NET note on 'their synagogues' (v.23) discusses the significance of the possessive pronoun: Matthew uses 'their synagogues' to mark a growing distance between Jesus's community and the Jewish establishment. The note traces this usage through Matthew and its theological implications.

heb.paragraph: The first discipleship word in Matthew. It means physically walking behind someone but carries the deeper sense of becoming an apprentice. When the fishermen 'follow' Jesus, they do not join a school; they abandon a livelihood. The cost is immediate and total.

A metaphor that redefines their existing skill. They already know nets, currents, patience, and timing. Now the catch changes. The image is not gentle gathering but active pursuit — fishing requires going where the fish are.

hist.context: The calling of the first disciples (vv.18–22) and the summary of Jesus's early ministry (vv.23–25) bookend each other. The disciples are called with a fishing metaphor that will govern their identity throughout the gospel; the summary describes a ministry of teaching, proclaiming, and healing — the three activities that characterise everything in chs.5–9. The geographic sweep of v.25 (Galilee, Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, Transjordan) anticipates the commission to "all nations" of 28:19.

refs.note: I will send for many fishermen — God's gathering of Israel. Jesus's "fish for people" call re-uses the prophetic metaphor.
The geographic spread of v.25 anticipates the Great Commission: the movement from Galilee to all nations begins here.

ppl.name: Satan
ppl.text: Attacks the Son's identity three times. Offers the goal without the means. Defeated by Scripture-grounded obedience.

ppl.name: Peter and Andrew
ppl.text: Leave their nets immediately. The call creates the response.

ppl.name: James and John
ppl.text: Leave boat and father. The cost of discipleship introduced from the first day.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God."Deut 8:3The original context: God's lesson to Israel in the wilderness about the manna. Jesus applies it to himself: life comes from God's word, not from miraculous self-provision. The answer to the tempter is the same word God gave Israel in their wilderness.

src.title: The structure of the temptations
src.quote: Three tests, three Deuteronomy answers. The pattern is deliberate: the Torah is the sufficient defence against the tempter. Jesus wields Scripture as the instrument of victory — not miraculous power.
src.note: The Deuteronomy-exam structure is the key to understanding the temptation narrative.

src.title: Capernaum
src.quote: A fishing village on the northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee, c.3km from the Jordan inlet. Jesus's base of operations throughout the Galilean ministry.
src.note: Archaeologically well-attested; excavations have uncovered a synagogue and a house identified with Peter.

rec.text: The three temptations have been analysed as the pattern of all human temptation since Origen — physical appetite, presumption, and the lust for power. Augustine, Luther, and subsequent theologians have used Matthew 4 as the anatomy of sin.
The four disciples' immediate response has been the model of radical discipleship from the first century monastic movement through Francis of Assisi to contemporary mission.

rows.label: The Temptation: Three Tests and One Pattern
rows.text: Bread, pinnacle, kingdoms -- each temptation offers a shortcut around the cross: provision without trust, protection without obedience, authority without surrender. Jesus refuses all three with Scripture.

rows.label: The Temptation: Three Tests and One Pattern
rows.text: Bread, pinnacle, kingdoms -- each temptation offers a shortcut around the cross: provision without trust, protection without obedience, authority without surrender. Jesus refuses all three with Scripture.

rows.label: Galilee of the Gentiles: Light at the Margin — 4:12-25
rows.text: Jesus inaugurates his ministry in the most Gentile-influenced region of Israel. The mission to the nations begins in the land of darkness. The geography is theological.

tx.title: 'He will command his angels concerning you' (Ps 91:11-12)
tx.content: The devil quotes Ps 91:11-12 accurately. Jesus does not dispute the text but the application.
tx.note: The devil's hermeneutic is correct quotation with wrong application — taking a promise of providential protection and turning it into a test of divine reliability. Scripture's promises govern the life of trust, not the manufacture of signs on demand.

positions.name: Genuinely real — Jesus could have yielded
positions.argument: If not genuinely possible to accept, it was not a real temptation. The fully human Jesus was genuinely tempted; the fully divine Son was incapable of sinning. The tension is the Christological mystery (Heb 4:15).

positions.name: Representative trial — Jesus as Israel's covenant head
positions.argument: Jesus as the true Israel faces and defeats the tests Israel failed in the wilderness. The typological significance gives the event its redemptive-historical meaning.

themes.note: Matthew 4 establishes Jesus' authority over the devil, his identification with the covenant tradition, and his mission's surprising geography. The governing insight: Jesus conquers the devil not by divine power displays but by covenant faithfulness to the word — the same weapon available to every disciple.

coaching.tip: Three temptations, three Scripture responses. Satan quotes Psalm 91; Jesus counters with Deuteronomy. The wilderness temptations mirror Israel's failures—bread (Exod 16), testing God (Exod 17), idolatry (Exod 32). Where Israel failed, Jesus stands.
'Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men' (v. 19). Immediate obedience: 'at once they left their nets' (v. 20). No negotiation, no delay. The call brooks no competitors.
